title: Chapter 1: The Corpus
text:
Corpus selection and preperation
The corpus for this analysis is a combination of two playlists from spotify: “West Side Classics” and “East Coast Classics”. These playlists were chosen, because they were very popular on spotify, with 141.754 and 40.882 likes respectivally. As there is no golden standard for what songs are considered “West Coast” or “East Coast”, this method of popularity was used, which is similair to methods used by Boros (2025) and Gilbers et al. (2019).
In the West Coast (W.C.) playlist, some popular East Coast (E.C.) artist appeard. To mitigate this problem, artist that were in both datasets were noted and placed in their corresponding Coast. The artist ‘Outkast’ also appeared in both datasets and was removed as Outkast originated from Atlanta (not W.C. nor E.C.). Moreover, “West Side Classics” contained 84 songs, while “East Coast Classics” contained 177 after the previous step of moving around songs. As this is a difference of more that half of the songs, the E.C. set was downsampled to the W.C. set, both now containing 84 songs.
After balancing the coasts, the corpora were combined. After that, an extra column region was added having either the value east or west corresponding to that rows coast.


Track selection
To make analysis about track level information, two tracks per coast were selected. These songs needed to be representative for the range of music of every coast. To select these without randomly choosing two, one is the most respresentative for that coast and the other is a track distinctive for its coast, far from the other coast and not an outlier in its own coast. The latter was chosen so that the most distinctive track from a coast is not a general outlier (i.e. the most distinctive song for a coast could be a song that does not represent either coasts).
First, all numerical features except “Popularity” (as this measure is changing) from the Spotify API were normalised. Second, the centroid (i.e. the mean over all features) of the data was calculated. To select the most representative song, the euclidean distance from each song to the centroid was calculated, and the song with the smallest distance was selected. To select the most distinctive song, the euclidean distance from each song to the other coast’s centroid was calculated. From that, the distance to its own coast’s centroid was subtracted. The song that was the furthest from the other coast’s centroid, while being close to its own coast’s centroid was selected. The songs that were selected are:






East Coast


West Coast




Representative


Distinct


Representative


Distinct






Give Me The Loot The Notorious B.I.G.


C.R.E.A.M. Wu-Tang Clan


Bitch Please Snoop Dogg & Xzibit


Without Me Eminem

title: Chapter 2: Exploratoring the features
text:
Before diving into songs specifically, we will look at whether there are differences on a coast level. To do this, we analyse a plot containing density-plots of both coast overlayed with eachother. This way, we can clearly see where the coasts’ features overlap and where not.









Overall, the distributions are quite similiar. Both coasts’ song distributions have relativaly the same spread of duration, energy, instrumentalness, key, liveness, loudness, mode, tempo and valence. Especially with a feature like tempo, it is striking to see how similair the two coasts are.
Next to that, there are three features where the distributions differ. First, acousticness show a clear seperation. W.C. songs are heavily skewed to less acoustic songs, while E.C. have a more spread acousticness distribution. Second, W.C. songs are more skewed toward a higher danceability than E.C. songs. Last, speechiness in W.C. songs is skewed to the left relative to E.C.
In addition, what is interstring to see is that the instrumentallness of all songs is zero (due to the fact that it is mostly rap, hip-hop and samples), while the speechiness remains also relatively low (around 0.3). This combination indicates that the songs have a more musical beat while still revoling around vocals, describing a rythmic speech style which resonates with rap.
Now that we have seen coast-level features, let’s dive into the four songs

title: Chapter 6: Temporal features
text:
Here, we dive into the tempo of the four songs. To make these tempograms, we have used both the autocorrelation (ATC) method, which is better at detecting lower BPM’s as the Direct Fourier Transform (DFT), which is better at higher tempo’s. The most typical songs are both slower, while the distinc songs are both faster.

Give Me The Loot - The Notorious B.I.G.   


Bitch Please - Snoop Dogg & Xzibit   










In this tempogram, you can see both the tempogram of Give Me The Loot and Bitch Please. In the tempogram of GMTL, you see the most clear line at 45 BPM and a darker line at around 90 BPM. The latter is a more realistic tempo for the song. In addition, the lines are bright at the start, but quickly become much darker. This is explainable by more samples coming into the song
The tempograph of Bitch Please picking up on more tempos, all quite as prominent as the other. When listening to the song, the tempo seems quite the same as in GMTL. When you look at the graphs side by side, you can see that the lines of GMTL and BP line up. BP also has a tempo of 95 BPM.

C.R.E.A.M. - Wu-Tang Clan   


Without Me - Eminem   










In this tempogram, you can see both the tempogram of C.R.E.A.M. and Without Me. CREAM picks up on a spread of beats. The most prominent is at around 180 BPM. However, when listening, half time of 90 BPM feels more natural.
The tempogram of without me is very clear. There are two lines, one at 220 BPM and one at 440 BPM. However, the actual BPM is at 120 BPM. The reason that the tempogram picks up on 220 BPM is probably due to the hi-hat, that hits on double speed.

Conclusion
To summerize, the both most typical track from each coast has a BPM below 100. Both distinctive tracks have a BPM higher than 100. Therefore, we can say that it is probable than a higher BPM is rap and hip-hop songs is generally more seen at the outliers of the genre and not at core. Here, the coasts seem to match.

title: Chapter 5: Timbre
text:
In this chapter, we take a loot at the timbre features of the four songs.

Give Me The Loot - The Notorious B.I.G.   


Bitch Please - Snoop Dogg & Xzibit   










In this figure, you see the cepstrograms of GMTL and BP. GMTL has a distinctive pattern. mfcc02 is bright yellow, just as mfcc04, while mfcc03 is very dark. This means the audio strongly aligns with the second and fourth coefficients but completely lacks the third. Because these horizontal bands barely fluctuate over time, the visual data tell us that the song relies on a highly consistent, looped sample. However, when listening to the song, it sounds less consistent, due to the many samples fading in and out. In the end of the songs, you hear gunshots. These are visible in the cepstrogram by the vertical stripe at the end.
On the other hand, BP has a more diverse timbre. Although the voices are quite steady, there are samples going in and out often. Interestingly, in the last 50 seconds of the track, the pattern stabilizes and a single instrumental loop becomes highly prominent, which explains why mfcc_03 suddenly becomes much darker and more consistent during that final frame.

C.R.E.A.M. - Wu-Tang Clan   


Without Me - Eminem   










In this figure, you see the cepstrograms of C.R.E.A.M. and Without Me. C.R.E.A.M. has a distinctive pattern. mfcc_02 is solid bright yellow, and mfcc_04 is also relatively bright, while mfcc_03 is dark. This means the audio strongly aligns with the second and fourth coefficients but largely lacks the third, just like in GMTL. Because these horizontal bands barely fluctuate over time, the visual data confirms that the song relies on a highly consistent, looped sample, giving it that steady, unchanging sonic tone. Which is obviously heard in this songs, in contrast to GMTL.
On the other hand, Without Me has a more diverse timbre. The continuous vertical striping across the lower coefficients shows a constantly shifting texture, meaning there are different instruments and rhythmic elements going in and out often. Interestingly, the rapidly alternating yellow and dark patterns in mfcc_02 and mfcc_03 throughout the entire track highlight a highly dynamic, bouncy production style, which explains why the visual data looks so much more fragmented and active compared to a single, static instrumental loop. This cepstrogram has similarities with BP, in the sense that there is a diverse timbre in both.

Conclusion
To summarize, both GMTL and CREAM have a steady timbre, which is apperantly distinctive for the E.C. BP and WM have a more diverse timbre, which seems to be distictive for the W.C.

title: Week 10
text:
Homework week 10
In the late 1990s and early 2000s, American hip-hop was marked by a West Coast vs. East Coast feud, with fans strongly aligned to one side. The rivalry ultimately led to the deaths of Tupac and Biggie. For this week, I made a chordagram for the song ‘Give me the Loot’ by The Notorius B.I.G. I chose this song as it is statistically the most east-coast, meaning its audio profile sits closest to the overall average of all East Coast tracks (Only considering spotify’s features). #### Listen to the song   









This is a chordagram of Give me the Loot by The Notorius B.I.G. In the chrodagram, you can see that the overall color is greenish, and that there are a lot of soft horizontal blue lines. The fact that the overall color is greenish and, for example, not bright yellow in most parts, tells us that the sound of this song is very rich. When listening to the song, there is a soft background sample overlayed with harder drums and ofcourse Biggies prominent voice. Together with that, there are a lot of sounds going in and out of the song: alarm like souds, record scratches and other voices. In the graph, you can also see a lot of vertical lines. Oftentimes in the song, you hear the samples and drums stop, only keeping Biggies voice. These stops correspond to the vertical lines, as there is a sudden stop of a lot of chords.

title: Week 11
text:
Homework week 11
In the late 1990s and early 2000s, American hip-hop was marked by a West Coast vs. East Coast feud, with fans strongly aligned to one side. The rivalry ultimately led to the deaths of Tupac and Biggie. For this week, I made a chordagram for the song ‘Give me the Loot’ by The Notorius B.I.G. I chose this song as it is statistically the most east-coast, meaning its audio profile sits closest to the overall average of all East Coast tracks (Only considering spotify’s features) (See here for more information).

Listen to Give me the Loot by The Notorius B.I.G.












In this graph, you see both the tempogram of Give Me The Loot made with Autocorrelation (ATC) and with the Discrete Fourier Transform. In both graphs, the lines are straight, indicating a steady rythm throughout the song. The tempo is around 90 BPM, which is a typical tempo for hip-hop/rap. In the ATC tempogram, the brightest line is prominent at around 45 BPM. In the DFT tempogram, the lines show at 90 BPM and multiples of that. Taking both the 45 BPM of the ATC and the 90 BPM of the DFT graph, we can conclude that the rythm of the song lies at around 90 BPM (45 BPM is half beat). Moreover, what stands out is that the tempo is prominent in the first 50 seconds, after which is fades out. If you listen closely in the song, the voice of Biggie get more prominent around the 50 second mark, resulting in less of a part of the output showing the beat, explaining this fading out phenomenon. Around the 100 seconds mark, there is a section where no tempo seems present. As you can hear in the song,the drums and samples are off around this time resulting in this gap in the tempo. In conclusion, this graph shows that this song has a steady beat with a tempo of around 90 BPM. This boom-bap-beat and tempo form the basis of the rapping, done by Biggies with his prominent voice.

title: Week 9
text:
Homework week 9

Introduction
In the late 1990s and early 2000s, American hip-hop was marked by a West Coast vs. East Coast feud, with fans strongly aligned to one side. The rivalry ultimately led to the deaths of Tupac and Biggie. For this week, I made visualisations for the song ‘Bitch please’ by Snoop Dogg and Xzibit. I chose this song as it is statistically the most west-coast, meaning its audio profile sits closest to the overall average of all West Coast tracks (Only considering spotifies features).


Chromagram



Bitch Please chromagram


In this chromagram, you can see that the song is mostly in the keys B and F#, with G being high in the beginning of the song, but becoming less prominant later. This repetitiveness of pitches (i.e. the B and F#) can be explained by the fact that most rap songs have a looped sample, which repeat throughout the whole song


SelfSM timbre features



Bitch Please SSM


In this self-similarity matrix, you can see that the song features a highly dense, checkerboard-like pattern across the entire track, with consistent horizontal and vertical stripes indicating regular, repeating intervals of high similarity. This intense repetitiveness in the song’s timbre can be explained by the fact that most rap songs, particularly from this era of West Coast hip-hop, are built on a continuous, looped instrumental beat, meaning the underlying drum textures, basslines, and instrumentation repeat almost entirely unchanged throughout the whole song.

title: Week 11
text:
For this weeks assignment, I have implemented two KNN models. One was trained on the orginal spotify features and one was trained on the Spotify features and 3 extra features. These are:
I found these extra features from a hit-song-predictor project and they seemed like a good addition to the features. First, energy_dancebility gives and indication of a ‘party factor’, for tracks that are both energetic and danceble.Second, Valence x energy gives an indication of positive vibes and upbeatness. Last, mood score indicates something about the overall emotional tone.

title: Week 11
section: KNN Classification Results without feature engineering
text:
KNN Classification Results without feature engineering
















Best k: 1
Here, you see a plot of the KNN prediction constructed with PCA and a confusion matrix of the predictions. In the plot, the ground truth is indicated with the shape and the classifiction with the color. The plot shows that the model is able to capture some of the variance in the data, but there is a lot of overlap between the two classes, indicating that the model is not performing very well. The confusion matrix confirms this, showing that there are a lot of misclassifications.

title: Week 11
section: KNN Classification Results with feature engineering
text:
KNN Classification Results with feature engineering
















Best k: 1
The model with the extra features show a non-significant improvement on the classification. That could be, because there is little difference between east and west coast, but it could also be due to the fact that the features add little extra as they are primarily focused around enery and valence. If these features are not imporant at all, adding additional features for this does not help.

title: Chapter 8: Contribution
text:
Conclusion
In this portfolio, we compared West- and East-side rap to find out whether their feud was not only rooted in culture, but also in the music itself. To answer this, we first looked at the distributions of Spotify’s features. We found that the coasts are similair on a lot of features, like loudness, liveness and key. Furthermore, we saw differences in three features: West-Coast music was more accoustic, more danceable and less speechy compared to East-Coast music.
After looking at the distributions, we looked at both the most typical and most distinctive song from each coast. From the chroma features, we concluded that E.C. tracks where more free in the use of pitch, compared to W.C. Moreover, when looking at the timbre features, we saw that E.C. tracks where steady in their timbre throughout the song, while W.C. tracks had a more diverese timbre. The temporal features showed similarity in both coasts, typical songs have a BPM less then 100, while the distinctive songs had a high BPM.
At last, we looked at whether clustering and classification model were able to classify East- and West-Coast songs. K-means clustering seemed unable to form meaningfull clusters (in relation to an East- and West-Coast classification). In addition, KNN-classification classified only about half of the songs correct on both coast. In the contrary, descision-tree classification yielded an accuracy of 78%, with its most decisive factors being Accousticness, Speechiness and Valence.
What we can conclude from this is that there are minor differences between the coasts on corpus level, with the biggest difference in Accousticness. On a tracklevel, there seemed to be more differences, which we especially saw in the steady E.C. timbre and fluctuating W.C. timbre.
So, the feud between the coasts was justified up to a certain level. However, there were also many similairities between the regions, maybe more than differences. To end this portfolio, maybe hardcore fans of one coast should be more open to the music of the other, as they might be surprised by the similairities between them.

Bibliography
Boros, Abel. Lyrics and Audio Analysis of US Westcoast and Eastcoast Hip-Hop Music Computer Science. 2025.
Gilbers, Steven, et al. “Regional Variation in West and East Coast African-American English Prosody and Rap Flows.” Language and Speech, vol. 63, no. 4, 4 Nov. 2019, pp. 713–745, journals.sagepub.com/doi/full/10.1177/0023830919881479#fn4-0023830919881479, https://doi.org/10.1177/0023830919881479. Accessed 9 Mar. 2026.

title: Portfolio Overview
text:
During the early to mid-1990s, the American hip-hop landscape was defined by an intense and highly publicized rivalry between the East Coast and the West Coast. Rooted in regional pride and fueled by competing record labels, the feud grew from a musical competition into a severe cultural conflict. The central figures of this divide were The Notorious B.I.G. (representing the East Coast) and Tupac Shakur (representing the West Coast).
What began as lyrical jabs and media provocation eventually escalated into real-world violence. This era of hip-hop was marked by an underlying tension that tragically culminated in the drive-by shootings and unsolved murders of Shakur in September 1996 and The Notorious B.I.G. just six months later in March 1997.
While the history, the media coverage, and the cultural tragedy of this era are heavily documented, this portfolio tries to answers the question whether this east-west feud was not only prevalent in culture, but also whether there was a real difference in the music itself.
First, we take a look at the corpus and the main spread of it’s features. Then, we dive deeper into four songs from the corpus, by looking at Chroma, Timbre, Tempo and loudness. After that, we will try to cluster and classify the songs, looking at whether machine learning models can seperate the coasts.

title: Chapter 3: Chroma features
text:
We first start out with chromafeatures. Take a look at the side by side chromagrams of the most typical songs of each coast.

Give Me The Loot - The Notorious B.I.G.   


Bitch Please - Snoop Dogg & Xzibit   










The difference between these two songs is quite big. “Give Me The Loot” is packed with sounds happening all over the place, while “Bitch Please” is much more straightforward and stable.
Listen to both songs in the windown 2:00-2:10. In GMTL, you hear a beat, a two voices, three or four samples and record scratches, all in 10 seconds! Eventhough this is an extreme window in the track, it illustrates the richness of the sound of this song. When listening to other parts of the song, you can hear a stable sample and Biggies voice. The sample form the basis of the song, hence the quite stable line at B-flat. Moreover, throughout the song, there are a lot of yellow spikes seen btween C-sharp and F-sharp. This is explainable by Biggies voice. It quite deep from itself (hence the lower notes) and has some spikes to higher notes, being the highest at the 150-180 seconds mark.
On the other hand, in BP, the song is more melodically stable. There is a beat and a sample that stays stable throughout the whole song. In the 2:00-2:10 window, you can hear two more samples that both fade in and out throughout the song, a long sliding synth and a synthesized triangle sound. These samples follow the same melodical pattern. The stair-like pattern you hear from these samples, cannot be seen in the chromagram. What you can see, is that were the sliding synth is prominent (120-140 seconds and from 180 untill the end of the song), the B and F-sharp pitches are more yellow, while the rest of the pitches get darker.









When comparing the Self-Similarity matrices, both songs show similar base patterns of beats and samples, but with different intensities. GMTL displays more bright yellow lines indicating greater pitch variation, while BP shows less intense patterns, reflecting its more stable melodic structure.

C.R.E.A.M. - Wu-Tang Clan   


Without Me - Eminem   










The difference between C.R.E.A.M (CREAM) and Without Me (WM) is a lot less aparent. The Pitches in CREAM remain very stable, being most prominent in F-sharp, A, and C-sharp. The melody throughout the whole track is essentially the same except for the 25 second intro. Impurities in the pitch classes can be explained by many different voices, as Wu-Tang Clan is a big rap group.
WM is also quite a rich songs, but stays more stable in pitch class than GMTL we saw earlier. It’s most intense in G and D, and also periodically in F. There is a melodical riff in the background (a oboe like sound) and goes down in steps periodically. This can not really be seen in the chromagram, which can be explained due to the fact that it is quite a background sample. Eminems voice, for example, is much more prominent.









The difference in the SSM’s is more clear. The SSM of CREAM has quite some repeating blocks, alternated with bright lines. This is, again, only explainable by the different voices heard.
The SSM of WM quite bright allover. You can see some blocks, but they are all quite small. Eminem has a very prominent, acticulated voice, explaining the scale of differences.

Conclusion
To conclude this chapter, we can see that W.C. songs are more stable in adhering to their pitch classes. E.C. songs differ: the chromagram of GMTL does not have a prominent stable pitch class, while CREAM does. So, maybe, E.C. songs are more free in their pitch use that W.C. songs.

title: Chapter 7: Classification & Clustering Analysis
text: In this chapter, we will look at clustering and multiple classification algoritms. Instead of identifying a coast by hand, it will be interesting to see whether there can be algorithmicly determined to which coast a song belongs. First, we will start with a small feature selection section. Then, we try K-Means clustering. After that, we try KNN, A single descision tree and Random Forrest.

title: Chapter 7: Classification & Clustering Analysis
text:
K-Means Clustering
K-Means clustering is an unsupervised learning technique that partitions the data into k distinct groups based on feature similarity. As we are trying to find out whether K-Means can distinguish the two coasts, we set the K to 2.

Clustering Visualization









This is the with PCA visualised result of K-Means clustering for E.C. and W.C. Before looking at metrics, you can see that the clustering did not do well on disecting east and west. The blue cluster is quite small in relation to the orange cluster and shapes differ throughout. Also in the orange cluster is a lot of differentiation.


Clustering Performance Metrics



K-Means Clustering Performance


Metric
Value




Silhouette Score
0.3402





The Silhouette Score ranges from -1 to 1, where higher values indicate better-separated clusters. Thus, the silhoutte score achieved indicates weak structure. Or, in other words, there is some structure in the data, but not a lot.

title: Chapter 7: Classification & Clustering Analysis
text:
K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) Classification
K-Nearest Neighbors is a supervised classification algorithm that predicts a song’s region by finding the k most similar songs in the training data and using majority voting. Through 5 fold cross-validation, we identified the optimal value of k that balances bias and variance.

KNN Predictions











KNN Performance Metrics



KNN Confusion Matrix

As you can see in the visualisation of the KNN-classifier, the model did not classify the data well. As be seen in the performance metric, the model only reached a precision, recall and accuracy of around 0.5. This also matches the confusion matrix, where you can see it classfies about half to the right coast and half to the other.

title: Chapter 7: Classification & Clustering Analysis
text:
Decision Tree Classification
Decision Trees provide an interpretable classification model by recursively partitioning the feature space based on feature values. The main advantage of this model is, is that it is very easy to interpret. In addition, this model is quite simple. As our KNN classifier did not have much succes, maybe a simpler model is a better fit.

Decision Tree Model











Decision Tree Performance Metrics



Decision Tree Classification Performance


Metric
Value




Cost Complexity Parameter
0.0000


Max Tree Depth
3.0000


Accuracy
0.7798


Precision (East Coast)
0.8052


Recall (East Coast)
0.7381





This graph visualizes the decision tree of the classifier. In the metric, you can see the tree scores an accuracy of 78%, which is quite high compared to the KNN classifier. In the graph, we can easily spot that the most important factor is Accousticness, which was already the most distictive feature we saw in chapter two. Valence and Speechiness were also identified. These results also match with the feature importance graph below. Dancebility, Tempo and Instumentalness were also to some importance, but apperantly not for the best tree.


Feature Importance









As this decision tree worked well, a Random Forrest model was also trained. However, results were not shared as the accuracy was below the accuracy of the KNN.


Conclusion
